summary: The Goshen Township Board of Trustees held their regular meeting covering the 2024 budget discussion across all departments including general fund, police, fire/EMS, and service. Key topics included tornado expense appropriations, cemetery mowing rebidding after the current contractor closed, disposal of obsolete police intoxilizers, personnel changes including a resignation and a sergeant promotion, and detailed budget presentations with discussions on vehicle purchases, road paving strategy, ARPA fund allocations, and healthcare cost increases. Judge Anita Beckham also introduced herself to the board as an incumbent municipal court judge running for election.
decisions: Motion to adopt the agenda - passed
Motion to approve payment of bills (Oct 11-23, 2023) - passed
Motion to increase appropriations for tornado expenses by $75,000 (fund 1000-110-590-0706) - passed
Motion to increase appropriation for lighting district electricity by $7,000 (fund 2401310351000) - passed
Motion to authorize township administrator to sign USDA rural development certificate of support for Goshen Schools - passed
Motion to declare surplus and dispose of two obsolete Intoxilyzer 5000 units from police department - passed
Motion to accept resignation of Justin Porterfield effective 10-11-23 - passed
Motion to accept resignation of part-time firefighter/EMT Aaron Port effective 10-22-23 - passed
Motion to approve out-of-town travel for Officer Tyler Smith for traffic crash reconstruction training (Jan 22 - Feb 2, 2024) - passed
Motion to adjourn into executive session at 7:16 PM - passed
Motion to promote Chris McMillan to police sergeant effective November 5, 2023 - passed
Motion to approve separation agreement with Travis Allen and authorize administrator to sign - passed
Motion to adjourn at 7:45 PM - passed

notes: The meeting included an extensive 2024 budget work session integrated into the regular meeting. The executive session lasted approximately 27 minutes (7:16-7:43 PM) and resulted in a police sergeant promotion and a separation agreement. The fire department is entering contract negotiations, so salary figures in the budget are preliminary. The township is exploring a new investment strategy expected to generate significantly more than the $30,000 budgeted in interest. There was discussion about whether HR costs can legally be transferred from department funds to the general fund - research confirmed transfers in to general fund are not permitted but costs can be directly charged to departmental funds. The UAW strike affecting Ford was noted as impacting police vehicle delivery timelines. The township spent approximately $900,000 on road paving in 2023 (covering roughly three years of work) and plans to skip 2024 paving to accumulate matching funds for a larger grant application in 2025.
title: BOT 10-24-23
